1:
‘Straightway in the morning the chief priests held a consultation… and bound Jesus… and delivered him to Pilate.’ The witnesses note the dawn ratification of the night’s illegal trial and the handing over to Rome.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the procedure. Robertson notes this was ‘the ratification meeting after day,’ their night action being illegal (compare Luke 22:66), and that Jesus, bound on his arrest (John 18:12), is bound again. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic rule that the great Sanhedrin sat from the morning sacrifice. Barnes refers to the full account in Matthew 27:1.

The fathers draw out the irony. Bede notes that, asking of no other charge but whether he was King of the Jews, they are ‘convicted of impiety, for they could not even find a false accusation.’ Theophylact observes they gave Jesus to the Romans, but ‘were themselves given up by God into the hands of the Romans’ (Psalms 28:5).

The Puritan Henry marks ‘the unwearied industry of wicked people’ — they met early and lost no time — shaming our slothfulness in good; and that Christ, the ‘great sacrifice,’ was bound with cords (Psalms 118:27). The shared, dominant note is that the Sanhedrin, having condemned Jesus by night, formally confirmed it at dawn and delivered him bound to Pilate, lacking the power to execute — so that the Lamb was led, bound, to the Gentile judge, the rulers’ haste and malice serving, unwittingly, the determinate counsel of God. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic rule that the great Sanhedrin sat from the morning sacrifice, and the witnesses agree the rulers, having condemned Jesus by night, confirmed it at dawn and delivered him bound to Pilate, lacking power to execute, so that the Lamb was led to the Gentile judge, their haste serving the determinate counsel of God. Bede notes the Jews could not even find a false accusation against the Saviour, asking of no charge but whether he was King of the Jews, so convicting themselves of impiety. Theophylact adds that they gave Jesus to the Romans but were themselves given up by God into Roman hands, that the Scripture might be fulfilled.
2:
‘Art thou the King of the Jews? And he answering said unto him, Thou sayest it.’ The two voices note the single charge Pilate weighs and Christ’s measured reply.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes this is ‘the only one of the charges… that he notices,’ for Pilate must attend to a claim of rivalry against Caesar though he believes Jesus ‘a harmless religious fanatic’ (John 18:33-37); ‘Thou sayest’ is an affirmation, the one question on which Christ’s testimony was essential. Gill notes the charge was the Jews’ suggestion of his crime.

With only two voices the synthesis is short, but they agree on the substance: of all the accusations, Pilate fastens on the political one — the claim to be King of the Jews — for that alone touched Roman interests, and Christ answers it with a guarded affirmation, ‘Thou sayest it.’ The grammatical commentators note the fuller interview in John, where Jesus explains that his kingdom is not of this world, so that Pilate sees he is no rival to Caesar.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ owns his kingship even before his judge, but in terms that disclaim all earthly rivalry. The ‘Thou sayest’ is no denial but an affirmation, yet so framed that Pilate perceives the kingdom in question is not political. The witnesses see in this both the truth of Christ’s royal claim, confessed in the hour of his deepest humiliation, and the wisdom that left Pilate without ground to condemn him as a seditious pretender, so that the governor would repeatedly seek to release him. Robertson notes the fuller interview in John, where Jesus explains his kingdom is not of this world, so Pilate sees he is no rival to Caesar; the witnesses agree Christ owns his kingship even before his judge, but in terms that disclaim earthly rivalry, leaving Pilate without ground to condemn him as a seditious pretender. Gill notes the charge was the Jews’ own suggestion of his crime, on which they desired sentence of death; the affirmation, though guarded, owned the truth of his royal claim before his judge.
3:
‘And the chief priests accused him of many things.’ The few voices note the multiplied accusations and Christ’s silence.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect — ‘repeated accusations… they let loose their venom against Jesus’ — the verb a common one for speaking against in court. Ellicott notes many manuscripts omit ‘but he answered nothing,’ the fact being implied in Pilate’s question and in v.5. Gill lists the charges: that he was a magician, a blasphemer, claimed to be the Son of God and a king, and forbade tribute to Caesar.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the chief priests pressed many accusations against Jesus, repeating and heaping up their charges. The grammatical commentators note the imperfect tense painting a continuous assault, and that the accusations — political and religious alike — were designed to secure his death.

The shared, dominant note is the contrast between the priests’ loud venom and Christ’s silence. They accused him of many things, but he answered nothing, fulfilling the prophecy of the Lamb dumb before its shearers (Isaiah 53:7). The witnesses see in the multiplied charges the malice of the rulers, who, unable to find one true accusation, multiplied false ones; and in Christ’s silence both his innocence, which needed no defence, and his willing submission to the suffering appointed him, as the silent Lamb led to the slaughter. Gill lists the charges — magic, blasphemy, claiming to be the Son of God and a king, forbidding tribute — and the witnesses agree the contrast between the priests’ loud venom and Christ’s silence fulfilled the prophecy of the Lamb dumb before its shearers (Isaiah 53:7), his silence showing both his innocence and his willing submission. Robertson notes the verb is a common one for speaking against in court, the imperfect painting a continuous assault of charges, political and religious alike, designed to secure his death. Ellicott notes many manuscripts omit ‘but he answered nothing,’ the fact being implied in Pilate’s question and in the marvelling that follows.
4:
‘Answerest thou nothing? behold how many things they witness against thee.’ The single voice notes Pilate’s renewed appeal.

The Reformed Gill notes that Pilate ‘asked him again’ in the presence of the accusing priests, the former question having been put when Jesus and he were alone in the judgment-hall, whither the Jews would not enter for fear of defilement (John 18:28).

With only one brief voice the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Pilate, astonished at the prisoner’s silence under so many charges, urges him again to answer, pointing to the multitude of accusations. The governor, willing as a judge to give the accused opportunity to defend himself, presses Jesus to reply.

The shared, dominant note, consistent with the surrounding verses, is the wonder of Pilate at Christ’s composure. The Roman judge, used to prisoners who pleaded eagerly for their lives, cannot understand a silence so complete under accusations so grave. His appeal — ‘answerest thou nothing?’ — is half a rebuke and half an invitation, hoping the prisoner will say something to relieve him of the burden of judgment. The witnesses see in the scene the fulfilment of prophecy: the Lamb opened not his mouth, and the very silence that baffled Pilate was the willing submission of the Saviour to the suffering decreed for the redemption of men. Gill notes the former question was put when Jesus and Pilate were alone in the hall, the Jews not entering for fear of defilement (John 18:28); the witnesses agree the Roman judge, used to prisoners pleading for their lives, could not understand a silence so complete, his appeal half a rebuke and half an invitation to answer. The witnesses agree the governor, willing as a judge to give the accused opportunity to defend himself, pressed Jesus to reply, hoping the prisoner would say something to relieve him of the burden of judgment. The appeal ‘answerest thou nothing?’ is half a rebuke and half an invitation, the silence fulfilling the prophecy of the Lamb that opened not its mouth. The Roman judge, used to prisoners who pleaded eagerly for their lives, could not understand a silence so complete under accusations so grave.
5:
‘But Jesus yet answered nothing; so that Pilate marvelled.’ The few voices note the silence that astonished the governor.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Pilate ‘was sure of the innocence of Jesus and saw through their envy’ (v.10), hoping he would answer to relieve him of the burden, and ‘marvelled also at the self-control of Jesus.’ Barnes ties the silence to the prophecy of the suffering servant (Isaiah 53:7, John 19:9). Gill notes Christ ‘made no defence for himself,’ though the governor, ‘as his friend, urged him.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Jesus continued silent under all the accusations, and Pilate marvelled. The grammatical commentators note the governor’s wonder was at both the innocence he perceived and the self-command of the prisoner, so unlike the desperate pleading he was used to.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s silence fulfilled the Scripture and manifested his majestic self-possession. As the Lamb dumb before its shearers, he opened not his mouth, neither defending himself against false charges nor courting the favour of his judge. The witnesses see in this both a witness to his innocence — for the guilty plead, but the innocent, conscious of a higher purpose, may be silent — and a token of his willing endurance, marvellous even to a hardened Roman, who saw in the calm of the accused a dignity that set him apart from every other prisoner. Barnes ties the silence to the prophecy of the suffering servant (Isaiah 53:7); the witnesses agree Christ’s silence both fulfilled the Scripture and manifested his majestic self-possession, neither defending himself against false charges nor courting his judge’s favour, a dignity marvellous even to a hardened Roman. Robertson notes Pilate marvelled at the self-control of Jesus, so unlike the desperate pleading of other prisoners, a wonder at both the innocence he perceived and the majesty of the silent King. Gill notes Christ made no defence for himself, though the governor, as his friend, urged him to it, the silence of innocence needing no plea.
6:
‘Now at that feast he released unto them one prisoner, whomsoever they desired.’ The witnesses note the Passover custom that gave Pilate a chance to free Jesus.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the custom. Robertson and Ellicott note the imperfect (‘used to release’) marks a regular usage; Lightfoot, with Gill, argues against the variant ‘every feast,’ the release suiting the Passover, which commemorated Israel’s release from Egypt (John 18:39).

The fathers draw out the tragedy and the type. Bede notes Pilate ‘furnished many opportunities for releasing Jesus,’ first contrasting a robber with the Just One; the Jews, choosing ‘a murderer instead of the Saviour,’ ‘deservedly lost their salvation… their country and their kingdom.’ Pseudo-Jerome reads the two goats — the scape-goat loosed (Barabbas, the devil’s part) and the Lord’s portion slain (Leviticus 16:7-10). Theophylact marks ‘the wickedness of the Jews, and the moderation of Pilate.’

The shared, dominant note is that the Passover custom of releasing a prisoner became Pilate’s means of seeking to free Jesus — and the occasion of the people’s fatal choice. The witnesses see in the custom a providential opportunity for the Just One’s release, which the rulers turned to his ruin by demanding a murderer instead; and the fathers read in the two prisoners a figure of the gospel itself, the guilty set free and the innocent slain in his stead, that sinners might go at liberty through the death of the Lamb. Theophylact marks the wickedness of the Jews and the moderation of Pilate, though he too was worthy of condemnation for not resisting the people; the witnesses agree the custom of releasing a prisoner became Pilate’s means of seeking to free Jesus and the occasion of the people’s fatal choice, the fathers reading in the two prisoners a figure of the gospel exchange. Lightfoot and Gill argue against the variant ‘every feast,’ the release suiting the Passover, which commemorated Israel’s deliverance from Egypt (John 18:39). Bede notes the Jews, choosing a murderer instead of the Saviour, deservedly lost their salvation, their country, and their kingdom, which they preferred to Christ.
7:
‘And there was one named Barabbas, which lay bound with them that had made insurrection… who had committed murder.’ The witnesses note the character of the man chosen over Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe him. Robertson calls him ‘a desperate criminal, leader in the insurrection… against Rome,’ bound with his fellow-rioters — the very revolution the Jews had once wanted Jesus to lead (John 6:15); ‘the priests and people actually chose a murderer in preference to Jesus.’ Ellicott notes Mark alone records that his fellow-insurgents were imprisoned with him; Lightfoot cites rabbinic notices of famous robbers. Wesley notes insurrection was a crime Pilate was especially careful to punish.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Barabbas was no petty thief but a notorious rebel and murderer, bound with the insurrectionists. The grammatical commentators stress the irony — the people demanded the release of a man guilty of the very sedition falsely charged against Jesus.

The shared, dominant note is the dark contrast between Barabbas and Christ. The one was a murderer and rebel, the other the innocent Saviour; yet the people, urged by their rulers, would have the guilty go free and the guiltless die. The witnesses see in this both the depth of the nation’s perversity, preferring a man of blood to the Prince of Life, and, beneath it, the providence of the gospel: that the guilty Barabbas, set at liberty while Jesus died, is a living emblem of every sinner who goes free because Christ took his place. Lightfoot cites rabbinic notices of famous robbers, and Wesley that insurrection was a crime Pilate especially punished; the witnesses agree the people, urged by their rulers, would have the guilty go free and the guiltless die, a dark perversity that is yet, beneath it, the providence of the gospel, the guilty Barabbas set free because Christ took his place. Robertson notes the priests and people actually chose a murderer in preference to Jesus, the very revolution against Rome that the Jews had once wanted Jesus himself to lead (John 6:15).
8:
‘And the multitude crying aloud began to desire him to do as he had ever done unto them.’ The few voices note the crowd’s clamour for the customary release.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the detail. Robertson and Ellicott note the imperfect — ‘as he was wont to do unto them’ — confirming the regular custom. The Wesleyan Clarke dwells on the word for ‘crying aloud,’ which ‘strongly marks the vociferations… the bellowing of the multitude,’ a loud and long cry. Gill notes some read ‘the people went up’ to the pavement where the judgment-seat was.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the crowd, gathering before Pilate, clamoured for him to keep the Passover custom and release a prisoner. The grammatical commentators note the imperfect confirming the practice, and Clarke the vehemence of the shouting, a foretaste of the uproar that would soon demand Christ’s death.

The shared, dominant note is the rising tumult of the multitude. They came demanding the customary release, and their loud clamour, the witnesses observe, was the instrument the rulers would seize and turn against Jesus. The very crowd that asked for a prisoner’s freedom would, when prompted, ask for Barabbas and the crucifixion of Christ; and the bellowing that Clarke marks is the voice of a fickle mob, soon to exchange its hosannas for the cry of ‘Crucify.’ Gill notes some read ‘the people went up’ to the pavement where the judgment-seat was; the witnesses agree the crowd’s loud clamour for the customary release was the instrument the rulers would seize and turn against Jesus, the same crowd that asked a prisoner’s freedom soon asking for Barabbas and the crucifixion of Christ. Clarke dwells on the word for ‘crying aloud,’ which marks the bellowing of the multitude, a loud and long cry, a foretaste of the uproar that would soon demand Christ’s death. Robertson and Ellicott note the imperfect ‘as he was wont to do unto them’ confirms the regular custom of the Passover release. Gill notes some read that the people went up to the pavement, where the judgment-seat was, to make their demand before the governor.
9:
‘Will ye that I release unto you the King of the Jews?’ The few voices note Pilate’s half-hopeful offer.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the form of the question ‘implies… a half hope of an affirmative answer’ (so John 18:39); Robertson that ‘the King of the Jews’ sharpens the contrast with Barabbas. Gill notes Pilate was ‘satisfied of the innocence of Jesus, and being willing to dismiss him.’

The Wesleyan Wesley asks what the ‘wretched man’ most discovers, ‘want of justice, or courage, or common sense?’ — the ‘poor coward sacrifices justice to popular clamour,’ and enrages the rulers by repeating the offensive title.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Pilate, convinced of Christ’s innocence, offered to release him under the Passover custom, hoping the people would take him rather than Barabbas. The grammatical commentators note the half-hope in his question and the pointed title ‘the King of the Jews,’ which both contrasted Jesus with the murderer and needled the rulers.

The shared, dominant note is the weakness of Pilate’s well-meant attempt. He wished to free Jesus, knew him innocent, and used the custom to that end; yet, as Wesley charges, he sought to do justice by appeasing the mob rather than by his own authority. The witnesses see in his offer both a genuine effort to save the innocent and the fatal flaw of a judge who, lacking courage, put the decision into the hands of a crowd already manipulated by the priests, so that his half-measure only hastened the very end he wished to avoid. Gill notes Pilate was satisfied of Christ’s innocence and willing to dismiss him; the witnesses agree his offer was both a genuine effort to save the innocent and the fatal flaw of a judge who, lacking courage, put the decision into the hands of a crowd already manipulated by the priests, so that his half-measure only hastened the end he wished to avoid. Wesley asks what the wretched man most discovers, want of justice or courage, the poor coward sacrificing justice to popular clamour and enraging the rulers by repeating the offensive title.
10:
‘For he knew that the chief priests had delivered him for envy.’ The few voices note Pilate’s discernment of the rulers’ motive.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect ‘he perceived’ — ‘descriptive of Pilate’s growing apprehension’, the truth ‘gradually dawning on him’; both Mark and Matthew give ‘envy’ as the primary motive, Pilate having likely heard of the triumphal entry and temple teaching. Barnes ties the envy to its biblical character (Proverbs 27:4, Acts 13:45). Gill notes the chief priests, especially Caiaphas.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Pilate saw through the rulers’ pretext — it was not zeal for Caesar but envy that had handed Jesus over. The grammatical commentators note the imperfect tense painting the dawning of this perception, fed by what Pilate had heard of Christ’s popularity.

The shared, dominant note is that envy was the root of the rulers’ malice, and Pilate knew it. They feared the loss of their authority as the people thronged to Jesus, and so, under a show of loyalty to Rome, sought his death. The witnesses see in this both the meanness of the motive — envy, which Scripture marks as cruel and consuming — and the added guilt of Pilate, who, perceiving their malice and the innocence of the accused, yet lacked the resolve to acquit him, and so became the instrument of an envy he himself despised. Gill notes the chief priests, especially Caiaphas; the witnesses agree envy was the root of the rulers’ malice, and Pilate knew it — they feared the loss of their authority as the people thronged to Jesus — so that his perceiving their malice and the prisoner’s innocence, yet lacking resolve to acquit, added to his guilt. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘he perceived’ paints the dawning of this conviction, fed by what Pilate had heard of Christ’s popularity at the triumphal entry and the temple teaching. Barnes ties the envy to its biblical character as cruel and consuming (Proverbs 27:4), the meanness of the motive adding to Pilate’s guilt.
11:
‘But the chief priests moved the people, that he should rather release Barabbas.’ The few voices note the rulers’ stirring up of the crowd.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the strong verb — ‘stirred up’ (shook up like an earthquake) — where Matthew 27:20 has the weaker ‘persuaded’; the priests had ‘amazing success,’ for this was not the same crowd of the triumphal entry but a mob got together before the Galilean sympathizers awoke. Barnes ties it to the rejection of the Holy One (Acts 3:14). Gill notes they ‘greatly solicited and persuaded’ the people.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the chief priests worked on the crowd to demand Barabbas, overturning Pilate’s hope. The grammatical commentators note the violence of the verb — the rulers shook the people into a frenzy — and the cunning timing, before Christ’s friends could gather.

The shared, dominant note is the decisive influence of the rulers over the fickle multitude. Pilate had offered the people their King; the priests, by vehement agitation, turned them to ask for a murderer instead. The witnesses see in this both the power of wicked leaders to corrupt a crowd — the same lips that had cried Hosanna now, stirred up, would cry Crucify — and the fulfilment of prophecy, that the Holy and Just One should be denied and a murderer granted in his place, the nation choosing its own ruin under the guidance of its blind guides. Gill notes they ‘greatly solicited and persuaded’ the people; the witnesses agree the same lips that had cried Hosanna now, stirred up, would cry Crucify, fulfilling the prophecy that the Holy and Just One should be denied and a murderer granted in his place, the nation choosing its ruin under its blind guides. Robertson notes the strong verb ‘stirred up’ (shook like an earthquake) where Matthew 27:20 has the weaker ‘persuaded,’ the priests having amazing success with a mob got together before the Galilean sympathizers awoke. Barnes ties the demand to the rejection of the Holy and Just One (Acts 3:14), the people preferring a man of blood to the Prince of Life.
12:
‘What will ye then that I shall do unto him whom ye call the King of the Jews?’ The few voices note Pilate’s renewed, pointed question.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Pilate ‘rubs it in on the Jews’ (cf. v.9), the ‘then’ meaning ‘since you have chosen Barabbas.’ Ellicott marks ‘the same determination to fasten upon the Jews the reproach that it was indeed their king whom he condemned,’ as later in the title on the cross (John 19:21-22). Gill notes Pilate was ‘astonished’ they would ask the release of so infamous a person. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Pilate, having lost on Barabbas, asks what then he shall do with ‘the King of the Jews,’ still pressing the title that galled the rulers. The grammatical commentators note his persistence in fastening on them the reproach that it was their own king they were condemning.

The shared, dominant note is Pilate’s pointed needling of the Jews even as he yields ground. By repeating ‘whom ye call the King of the Jews,’ he both reminds them of their guilt and presses them to declare their will. The witnesses see in this the same spirit as the inscription he would set on the cross and refuse to alter: a Gentile judge, half in scorn, forcing the nation to own that the man they doomed was their king — a reproach true beyond his knowing, for the crucified was indeed the King of Israel. Ellicott marks the same determination to fasten on the Jews the reproach that it was their king he condemned, as later in the title on the cross (John 19:21-22); the witnesses agree Pilate, half in scorn, forced the nation to own that the man they doomed was their king, a reproach true beyond his knowing. Robertson notes the ‘then’ means ‘since you have chosen Barabbas,’ Pilate pressing the title that galled the rulers and forcing them to declare their will against their own king. Ellicott marks the same spirit as the title Pilate set on the cross and refused to alter, fastening on the Jews the reproach that it was their king he condemned.
13:
‘And they cried out again, Crucify him.’ The two voices note the mob’s demand for crucifixion.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘there was a chorus and a hubbub of confused voices all demanding crucifixion,’ and that ‘some of the voices beyond a doubt had joined in the hallelujahs… in the triumphal entry.’ Gill notes they had cried so once before, as Luke 23:21 relates.

With only two voices the synthesis is short, but they agree on the substance: the crowd, stirred by the priests, shouted for Christ to be crucified — not merely killed but put to the slave’s and rebel’s death of the cross. The grammatical commentators note the confused clamour of many voices, some belonging to the very people who days before had hailed him as the Son of David.

The shared, dominant note is the terrible fickleness of the multitude and the cruelty of their demand. ‘Crucify him’ — the cry rose against one convicted of no crime, from a crowd that had lately welcomed him with palms. The witnesses see in this both the warning of how quickly popular favour turns, when wicked leaders work upon a thoughtless crowd, and the depth of Christ’s humiliation: the King of the Jews, offered to his people, was answered with the demand for the most shameful and agonising of deaths, that he might bear, even in the manner of his dying, the curse of those he came to save. Gill notes they had cried so once before, as Luke 23:21 relates; the witnesses agree the cry ‘Crucify him’ rose against one convicted of no crime, from a crowd that had lately welcomed him with palms, a warning of how quickly popular favour turns when wicked leaders work upon a thoughtless crowd. Robertson notes a chorus and hubbub of confused voices all demanding crucifixion, some of whom beyond doubt had joined the hallelujahs of the triumphal entry only days before. Gill notes the cruelty of the demand for crucifixion, the slave’s and rebel’s death, against one convicted of no crime. The same lips that had welcomed him with palms now, stirred up by the priests, demanded the most shameful and agonising of deaths.
14:
‘Why, what evil hath he done? And they cried out the more exceedingly, Crucify him.’ The two brief voices note Pilate’s vain protest and the mob’s fury.

The Reformed Gill notes this was ‘the third time’ Pilate spoke in Christ’s defence, and the grammatical-historical Barnes ties the governor’s own testimony — that no evil was found — to the innocence of the suffering servant (Isaiah 53:9).

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Pilate, still seeking to save Jesus, asks what crime he has committed; but the crowd, past reason, only shouts the louder for his crucifixion. The judge’s question is itself a testimony to Christ’s innocence, for he could name no evil deed.

The shared, dominant note is the contrast between Pilate’s feeble appeal to reason and the mob’s unreasoning rage. ‘What evil hath he done?’ — the question admits of no answer, for none could be given; yet the people, inflamed by their rulers, answered only with a fiercer cry. The witnesses see in this both a fresh attestation of Christ’s sinlessness, wrung from the lips of his judge, and the irrationality of the hatred against him — a hatred that, having no just ground, fed on its own fury and would be satisfied with nothing less than the cross. The witnesses agree Pilate’s question ‘what evil hath he done?’ admits of no answer, for none could be given, yet the people answered only with a fiercer cry; they see in this both a fresh attestation of Christ’s sinlessness, wrung from his judge, and the irrationality of a hatred that, having no just ground, fed on its own fury. Gill notes this was the third time Pilate spoke in Christ’s defence, his very question a testimony to the innocence of the suffering servant (Isaiah 53:9), for he could name no evil deed. The crowd, past reason and inflamed by their rulers, only cried the louder, a hatred that, having no just ground, fed on its own fury. Pilate’s feeble appeal to reason was met by the mob’s unreasoning rage, a fresh attestation of Christ’s sinlessness wrung from his judge.
15:
‘And so Pilate, willing to content the people… delivered Jesus… to be crucified.’ The witnesses note the unjust sentence, the scourging, and the release of Barabbas.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘to content the multitude’ is a Latin idiom — to do what removes one’s ground of complaint — and that Pilate, afraid of the crowd and of what they would tell Caesar, yielded; the scourging preceded crucifixion. Gill notes Pilate did this ‘to humour them, and keep in their favour.’

The Puritan Henry marks the exchange: he ‘released Barabbas… the scandal… of their nation, and delivered Jesus… the glory and blessing of their nation,’ and that, having scourged him, ‘he went on’ to crucify, for he who could chastise the innocent could by degrees crucify him. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, dwells on the smiting and spitting of Isaiah 50:6 and Isaiah 53:1.

The shared, dominant note is the final injustice: a judge who knew the prisoner innocent, to please the mob, scourged him and gave him up to the cross while freeing a murderer. The witnesses see in Pilate the type of all who sacrifice conscience to expediency, and in the scourging and sentence the willing submission of the Saviour, who, numbered with the transgressors, bore the unjust condemnation that the guilty might be acquitted. Robertson notes Pilate, afraid of the crowd and of what they would tell Caesar, yielded, the scourging preceding crucifixion; the witnesses see in Pilate the type of all who sacrifice conscience to expediency, and in the scourging and sentence the willing submission of the Saviour, numbered with the transgressors, who bore unjust condemnation that the guilty might be acquitted. Henry marks the exchange — Barabbas the nation’s scandal released, Jesus its glory delivered up — and that he who could chastise the innocent could by degrees be brought to crucify him. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, dwells on the smiting and spitting that fulfilled Isaiah 50:6. Robertson notes the idiom ‘to content the multitude’ means to do what removes their ground of complaint, Pilate yielding from fear of the crowd and of Caesar.
16:
‘And the soldiers led him away into the hall, called Praetorium; and they call together the whole band.’ The witnesses note the soldiers’ mockery of the King.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place. Robertson and Ellicott note ‘Praetorium’ is a Latin word for the governor’s palace (so Matthew 27:27), the ‘band’ being the cohort gathered into the court. Wesley calls it the inner hall.

The fathers read the mockery as unwitting truth. Bede notes that, since he was called King, they in derision strip him and clothe him in royal purple, crowning him with thorns and giving a reed for a sceptre. Pseudo-Jerome: ‘his shame took away our shame… by the thorny crown… we have obtained the crown of the kingdom.’ Theophylact bids us ‘put on the purple and royal robe… treading on serpents,’ and the crown of thorns of ‘a strict life.’

The shared, dominant note is that the soldiers’ cruel sport became, against their will, a true coronation. They gathered the whole band to mock a King; yet the fathers see in the purple and the thorns the deep mystery of the gospel — that by bearing shame Christ won for his people the crown of glory, and that his mock-royalty was the veiled manifestation of his real kingship. The witnesses agree that the indignities heaped on him in the Praetorium were both the lowest point of his humiliation and the occasion of a homage truer than the mockers knew. Robertson and Ellicott note the ‘band’ was the cohort gathered into the court of the palace; the witnesses agree the soldiers’ cruel sport became, against their will, a true coronation, the fathers seeing in the purple and the thorns the deep mystery of the gospel — that by bearing shame Christ won for his people the crown of glory. Bede notes that, since he was called King, they in derision strip him and clothe him in royal purple, crowning him with thorns and giving a reed for a sceptre, bowing the knee as to a king. Pseudo-Jerome notes his shame took away our shame, the thorny crown winning for us the crown of the kingdom, and by his wounds we are healed.
17:
‘And they clothed him with purple, and platted a crown of thorns, and put it about his head.’ The witnesses note the royal colour and the crown, and debate its nature.

The grammatical-historical commentators reconcile the colours. Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson and Wesley note that Mark and John say ‘purple,’ Matthew ‘scarlet’ (Matthew 27:28), the Tyrian purple being near scarlet, likely an old soldier’s coat used as a mock robe; the crown was set ‘in the form of a garland’ about the temples. Clarke, citing Bishop Pearce, doubts whether the ‘thorns’ were the torturing kind we imagine, the word perhaps meaning the acanthus plant.

The Reformed Gill notes the purple was ‘a mock purple robe… one of the soldiers’ old coats,’ worn by kings, put on him in derision.

With so brief a body of genuine debate the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the soldiers arrayed Jesus in a royal robe and crowned him with thorns, mocking his kingship. The grammatical commentators harmonise the ‘purple’ and ‘scarlet’ as one near colour, and note the open question, raised by Clarke, of what sort of thorns were used.

The shared, dominant note is the bitter irony of the mock regalia. The robe of royalty and the crown — emblems of honour — were turned into instruments of scorn and pain; yet the older writers see in them, as at the previous verse, the veiled truth of Christ’s kingship and the price of our crown. The witnesses agree that, whatever the precise plant, the crown of thorns marked the King who took upon himself the curse of the ground (Genesis 3:18), wearing our thorns that we might wear his crown. Clarke, citing Bishop Pearce, doubts the ‘thorns’ were the torturing kind we imagine, the word perhaps meaning the acanthus; the witnesses agree the robe of royalty and the crown were turned into instruments of scorn, yet the older writers see in them the veiled truth of Christ’s kingship, the King wearing our thorns (Genesis 3:18) that we might wear his crown. Gill notes the purple was a mock robe, likely one of the soldiers’ old coats, worn by kings and put on him in derision, the crown set as a garland about the temples.
18:
‘And began to salute him, Hail, King of the Jews!’ The single voice notes the mock homage.

The Reformed Gill explains they saluted him ‘in a mock way, wishing him long life and prosperity, as if he was a king just come to his throne, and this was his coronation day.’ The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact concerns the epileptic boy and so does not bear on the verse.

With only one brief and relevant voice the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain. The soldiers, having robed and crowned him, now bow in feigned reverence and hail him as King of the Jews — aping the salutation due to a sovereign at his accession, but in derision.

The shared, dominant note, consistent with the surrounding verses, is the cruel parody of royal honour. The acclamation ‘Hail, King of the Jews’ was meant as scorn, a mockery of his claim; yet, like the purple and the thorns, it spoke a truth the mockers did not intend, for he whom they derided was indeed the King. The witnesses see in the soldiers’ sport the depth of Christ’s humiliation — the Lord of glory made the butt of a barrack-room jest — and, beneath it, the dignity that no mockery could touch, the King receiving in scorn the title that was his by right, on the very day he won his kingdom by his blood. The witnesses agree the soldiers, having robed and crowned him, bowed in feigned reverence and hailed him King of the Jews in derision, a cruel parody that yet spoke a truth the mockers did not intend, for he whom they derided was indeed the King, receiving in scorn the title that was his by right on the very day he won his kingdom by his blood. Gill explains they saluted him in a mock way, wishing him long life and prosperity, as if he were a king just come to his throne and this his coronation day. The acclamation, meant as scorn, spoke a truth the mockers did not intend, for he whom they derided was indeed the King by right.
19:
‘And they smote him on the head with a reed, and did spit upon him, and bowing their knees worshipped him.’ The few voices note the repeated indignities.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the verbs. Robertson and Ellicott note all three — smote, spat, worshipped — are in the tense ‘which implies frequent repetition,’ the ‘worship’ being mock homage to one they regarded as a foolish pretender, not religious adoration. Barnes adds the word denotes only the respect done to princes. Gill notes the reed they had given for a sceptre they took back to ‘drive the sharp pointed thorns into his temples.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the soldiers heaped repeated abuse on Jesus — striking his thorn-crowned head, spitting on him, bending the knee in mock worship. The grammatical commentators note the imperfect tenses painting a sustained mockery, and that the ‘worship’ was the sham homage of derision.

The shared, dominant note is the prolonged cruelty and scorn poured on the Saviour. Blow after blow, the spittle, the mocking genuflections — all the indignities a brutal soldiery could devise were visited on the silent King. The witnesses see in this the fulfilment of prophecy, that he gave his back to the smiters and hid not his face from shame and spitting (Isaiah 50:6); and in the mock worship a dark foreshadowing of the true worship every knee shall one day render him (Philippians 2:10), the soldiers bowing in jest before the One before whom they must at last bow in earnest. Gill notes the reed given for a sceptre they took back to drive the thorns into his temples; the witnesses see in the prolonged cruelty the fulfilment of prophecy, that he gave his back to the smiters and hid not his face from shame and spitting (Isaiah 50:6), and in the mock worship a dark foreshadowing of the true worship every knee shall render him (Philippians 2:10). Barnes notes the ‘worship’ denotes only the homage done to princes, here in mockery, they regarding him as a foolish pretender to a kingdom, not as claiming to be divine.
20:
‘And when they had mocked him… led him out to crucify him.’ The witnesses note the end of the mockery and the procession to the cross, with the fathers’ rich typology.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the vivid historical present ‘they lead him out’ after the imperfects of mockery; Augustine harmonises the ‘wine mingled with myrrh’ with Matthew’s ‘gall’ and addresses the hour-discrepancy. They put his own clothes back on him for the road (Matthew 27:31).

The fathers read the way of the cross through the Old Testament. Pseudo-Jerome heaps up the types: Abel led to the field, Isaac with the wood, Moses with the rod and the serpent on the pole, the cluster of grapes on a staff — all foreshadowing the cross by which fallen man ‘swims to paradise.’ Bede reads Simon the Cyrenian, ‘obedient’ and an ‘heir,’ as the Gentiles taking up the cross after Jesus. Theophylact notes Christ first bore his own cross (John 19:17) till Simon was compelled.

The shared, dominant note is that, the mockery done, Christ was led out to be crucified, bearing his cross toward Golgotha. The witnesses see in the procession both the historical end of the soldiers’ sport — his own garments restored, the road begun — and, with the fathers, a tapestry of Old Testament shadows fulfilled in the cross-bearing Saviour, the wood of Isaac and the serpent on the pole finding their truth in the Lamb led out to die. Augustine harmonises the wine mingled with myrrh with Matthew’s gall and addresses the hour-discrepancy; the witnesses see in the procession both the historical end of the soldiers’ sport, his own garments restored, and the fathers’ tapestry of Old Testament shadows fulfilled in the cross-bearing Saviour, the wood of Isaac and the serpent on the pole finding their truth in the Lamb led out to die. Bede reads Simon the Cyrenian, interpreted ‘obedient’ and ‘heir,’ as the Gentile nations taking up the cross after Jesus, leaving pagan rites to follow the footsteps of his Passion. Pseudo-Jerome heaps up the Old Testament types — Isaac with the wood, the serpent on the pole, the grape-cluster on a staff — all foreshadowing the cross.
21:
‘And they compel one Simon a Cyrenian… the father of Alexander and Rufus, to bear his cross.’ The witnesses note the pressed cross-bearer and his named sons.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the detail. Robertson notes the Persian word for ‘compel’ (impress into service, Matthew 5:41), Simon ‘coming out of the country’ and meeting the procession; Jesus bore his own cross till relieved. Ellicott and Clarke mark that the naming of ‘Alexander and Rufus’ — a detail peculiar to Mark — implies they were known to his readers; the Rufus of Romans 16:13 is plausibly the same, linking Mark’s Gospel to Rome. Lightfoot notes Simon came in from the field.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the soldiers impressed a passing Cyrenian, Simon, to carry the cross when Jesus could bear it no farther. The grammatical commentators dwell on the named sons, a touch that betrays the eyewitness and the Roman audience, the family being later eminent among the Christians.

The shared, dominant note is that an unwilling stranger was made to share the cross of Christ — and, the fathers add, became thereby a figure of the Gentile believer. The naming of Alexander and Rufus, the witnesses observe, suggests that Simon’s forced service issued in his family’s faith, so that the cross he was compelled to bear became his honour. They see in him both a historical particular of the Passion and a pattern of discipleship: to take up the cross and follow after Jesus. Ellicott notes the naming of Alexander and Rufus, peculiar to Mark, implies they were known to his readers, the Rufus of Romans 16:13 plausibly the same, linking the Gospel to Rome. Robertson notes the Persian word for ‘compel’ means to impress into service (Matthew 5:41), Jesus bearing his own cross till relieved by Simon.
22:
‘And they bring him unto the place Golgotha… The place of a skull.’ The witnesses note the place of crucifixion and its name.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the historical present ‘they bring him,’ and refers to the discussion of Golgotha (Matthew 27:33). Gill notes it was ‘a famous, or rather an infamous’ place of execution.

The Puritan Henry notes it was ‘the common place of execution,’ so that he was ‘in all respects numbered with the transgressors,’ and reports the ancient tradition (in Tertullian, Origen, Chrysostom) that Adam was buried there — ‘highly congruous… for as in Adam all die, so in Christ shall all be made alive’ (1 Corinthians 15:22).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Jesus was brought to Golgotha, ‘the place of a skull,’ the common ground of execution outside the city. The grammatical commentators note the vivid present tense, and Henry the fitness of the place — among the carcases of criminals, the Holy One was numbered with the transgressors.

The shared, dominant note is the shame and significance of the place. Golgotha was a place of death and reproach, the haunt of executions; there the Prince of Life was led to die. The witnesses see in this both the depth of his humiliation — crucified at the common gallows, outside the camp (Hebrews 13:12) — and, in the old tradition of Adam’s grave, a fitting symbol: that where the first man’s death lay, the second Adam died to bring life, the skull-strewn hill becoming the place of the world’s redemption. Robertson refers to the discussion of Golgotha (Matthew 27:33); the witnesses see in the shame of the place both the depth of his humiliation — crucified at the common gallows, outside the camp (Hebrews 13:12) — and, in the old tradition of Adam’s grave, a fitting symbol, that where the first man’s death lay, the second Adam died to bring life. Henry notes it was the common place of execution, so that he was in all respects numbered with the transgressors, and reports the ancient tradition that Adam was buried there.
23:
‘And they gave him to drink wine mingled with myrrh: but he received it not.’ The few voices note the stupefying draught Christ refused.

The grammatical-historical commentators reconcile the accounts. Barnes and Ellicott note Matthew’s ‘vinegar’/‘gall’ (Matthew 27:34) and Mark’s ‘wine mingled with myrrh’ describe the same bitter, drugged drink; Robertson notes the perfect participle ‘myrrhed wine,’ and that Jesus ‘received it not,’ refusing it. Gill notes wine with frankincense was ‘usually given by the Jews to persons going to die,’ to dull the senses.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: at Golgotha Jesus was offered the customary stupefying draught — wine drugged with myrrh — but, having tasted it, would not drink. The grammatical commentators harmonise the various names of the drink and note that Christ deliberately refused its dulling effect.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ chose to meet his sufferings with unclouded mind. The drink was a mercy offered to the condemned, to deaden pain; but the Saviour refused it, that he might drink to the dregs the cup his Father gave him and bear the full weight of his Passion in full consciousness. The witnesses see in his refusal both the completeness of his sacrifice — no part of the suffering evaded — and his resolve to endure the cross awake to all its agony, that nothing might be wanting in the obedience by which he redeemed his people. Gill notes wine with frankincense was usually given by the Jews to the dying to dull the senses; the witnesses agree Christ chose to meet his sufferings with unclouded mind, refusing the dulling draught that he might drink to the dregs the cup his Father gave him and bear the full weight of his Passion in full consciousness. Barnes and Ellicott note Matthew’s ‘vinegar’ and ‘gall’ (Matthew 27:34) and Mark’s ‘wine mingled with myrrh’ describe the same bitter, drugged drink, which Christ tasted but would not drink. Gill notes the drink was given by the Jews to those going to die, to dull the senses, which Christ refused that he might bear his Passion with unclouded mind.
24:
‘And when they had crucified him, they parted his garments, casting lots upon them, what every man should take.’ The few voices note the crucifixion and the dividing of the clothes.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘what each should take’ (a double interrogative) is ‘only in Mark,’ the details followed closely by Matthew 27:35-44; the parting of the garments fulfilled Psalms 22:18. Barnes ties it to the same Psalm. Wesley notes Mark seems to intimate they nailed him to the cross, then parted his garments, then reared the cross up. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: they crucified Jesus and divided his garments by lot. The grammatical commentators note Mark’s vivid double question — who should take what — and that the soldiers’ casual gambling fulfilled the ancient prophecy of the Psalm to the letter.

The shared, dominant note is the restraint of the Gospel and the fulfilment of Scripture. Mark passes over the agony of the nailing in a single clause — ‘when they had crucified him’ — and dwells instead on the soldiers parting his clothes, the one poor possession of the dying. The witnesses see in the casting of lots both a mark of his utter stripping and destitution and a precise fulfilment of Psalms 22:18, written a thousand years before: the very indifference of the soldiers, gambling at the foot of the cross, served, all unknowing, to seal the truth of prophecy. Wesley notes Mark seems to give the order — nailing, parting the garments, then rearing the cross; the witnesses agree the soldiers’ casual gambling at the foot of the cross fulfilled Psalms 22:18 to the letter, the very indifference of the soldiers serving, all unknowing, to seal the truth of prophecy written a thousand years before. Robertson notes ‘what each should take’ (a double interrogative) is only in Mark, the details followed closely by Matthew 27:35-44, Mark passing over the agony of the nailing in a single clause. Robertson notes the parting of the garments fulfilled Psalm 22:18, the soldiers’ casual gambling sealing, all unknowing, the truth of prophecy.
25:
‘And it was the third hour, and they crucified him.’ The witnesses grapple with the famous discrepancy between Mark’s third hour and John’s sixth.

The grammatical-historical commentators face the crux. Robertson notes ‘the third hour’ is Jewish time, nine A.M., while the trial before Pilate was the ‘sixth hour’ Roman time, six A.M. (John 19:14). Barnes and Clarke explain the Jewish division of the day into four three-hour parts, each named from its first hour, so ‘the third hour’ covers from nine to noon. Lightfoot canvasses the difficulty at length.

The Reformed Calvin gives the same solution: the day was divided into four parts, each named from its commencement, so John, seeing Pilate wearing out the time as the hour of noon approached, reckoned by the larger division. Ellicott notes the precise hour is peculiar to Mark.

The shared, dominant note is that the two evangelists, rightly understood, do not contradict but reckon by different conventions. Mark uses Jewish hours (the third, nine A.M.) and John either Roman time or the loose naming of the day’s quarters. The witnesses agree the apparent conflict, long urged by sceptics, dissolves once the ancient modes of counting time are understood; and the point that stands is the plain, dread fact — ‘they crucified him’ — the hour fixed precisely by Mark so that the darkness at the sixth hour and the death at the ninth fall into their solemn order. Lightfoot canvasses the difficulty at length; the witnesses agree the two evangelists, rightly understood, do not contradict but reckon by different conventions — Mark by Jewish hours, John by Roman time or the loose naming of the day’s quarters — so the apparent conflict, long urged by sceptics, dissolves once the ancient modes of counting are understood. Barnes and Clarke explain the Jewish division of the day into four three-hour parts, each named from its first hour, so that ‘the third hour’ covers the time from nine to noon. Calvin gives the same solution, that John, seeing Pilate wearing out the time as noon approached, reckoned by the larger division of the day.
26:
‘And the superscription of his accusation was written over, THE KING OF THE JEWS.’ The few voices note the inscription on the cross.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the ‘superscription’ (our word epigraph) was the writing on the top of the cross, called ‘accusation’ by Matthew 27:37 and ‘title’ by John 19:19; Ellicott notes Mark gives the shortest form. Gill renders it ‘the cause of his death.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: over the cross was set the charge for which Jesus was condemned — ‘The King of the Jews.’ The grammatical commentators note the varied forms in the Gospels, Mark giving the briefest, and that it served as the legal statement of his crime.

The shared, dominant note is that the inscription, meant as a charge and a mockery, proclaimed an unwitting truth. The only ‘crime’ the title could name was that he was the King of the Jews — which he truly was. The witnesses see in the superscription, set up by Pilate and refused alteration at the priests’ protest (John 19:21-22), a providential testimony: in the three tongues of the empire, over the head of the crucified, was published the very confession his people denied, that the man dying there was their King, reigning, as the fathers loved to say, from the tree. Ellicott notes Mark gives the shortest form of the inscription; the witnesses see in the superscription, set up by Pilate and refused alteration at the priests’ protest (John 19:21-22), a providential testimony published over the crucified, that the man dying there was their King, reigning, as the fathers loved to say, from the tree. Robertson notes the ‘superscription’ (our word epigraph) is called ‘accusation’ by Matthew 27:37 and ‘title’ by John 19:19, the legal statement of his crime. Ellicott notes Mark gives the shortest form, the inscription proclaiming an unwitting truth, that the man dying there was the King of the Jews. The title, set up by Pilate and refused alteration at the priests’ protest, published over the crucified the very confession his people denied.
27:
‘And with him they crucify two thieves; the one on his right hand, and the other on his left.’ The few voices note the robbers crucified beside Christ.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects ‘thieves’ to ‘robbers’ (so Matthew 27:38). Clarke records the apocryphal tradition of their names, Zoathon and Chammatha. Gill notes they were crucified with him ‘for his greater reproach.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: two robbers were crucified with Jesus, one on either side. The grammatical commentators note the better rendering — robbers, not mere thieves — and that their placing, with Christ in the midst, was designed to heap further shame upon him.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ was crucified between two malefactors, numbered with the transgressors. The placing was an added indignity, as though he were the chief of criminals; yet it fulfilled the prophecy of the suffering servant and displayed, the witnesses observe, the very purpose of his death — that he died among and for sinners. The two robbers, one of whom would turn to him in faith, frame the cross as a picture of the gospel itself: the Saviour in the midst, and on either hand the guilty, divided by their response to him into the saved and the lost. Clarke records the apocryphal tradition of the robbers’ names; the witnesses agree the two robbers, one of whom would turn to Christ in faith, frame the cross as a picture of the gospel itself: the Saviour in the midst, and on either hand the guilty, divided by their response to him into the saved and the lost. Ellicott corrects ‘thieves’ to ‘robbers’ (Matthew 27:38), and Gill notes they were crucified with him for his greater reproach, as though he were the chief of criminals. The two robbers, one of whom would turn to Christ in faith, frame the cross as a picture of the gospel, the Saviour in the midst between the saved and the lost. Gill notes they were crucified with him for his greater reproach, as though he were the chief of the malefactors, numbered with the transgressors.
28:
‘And the scripture was fulfilled, which saith, And he was numbered with the transgressors.’ The witnesses note the citation and its textual standing.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the text. Clarke and Ellicott note the verse is ‘wanting in nearly all the better MSS,’ probably ‘originated in a marginal note’ from Luke 22:37; Ellicott observes it would be a rare instance of Mark dwelling on fulfilled prophecy. Barnes expounds the sense of Isaiah 53:12: not that Christ was a transgressor, but that ‘in dying he had a place with transgressors,’ dying ‘the just for the unjust.’

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, treats the two robbers — one ‘a transgressor to the end,’ the other finding in his cross ‘the beginning of restoration.’ With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the crucifying of Jesus between robbers fulfilled Isaiah’s word that the servant should be ‘numbered with the transgressors’ — though many hold the verse a later insertion in Mark.

The shared, dominant note is the meaning of the prophecy, whatever its place in the text. Christ was reckoned among criminals not because he was one but because he bore the sin of others; in his death, as in his life, he was holy, harmless, undefiled, yet he took the sinner’s place. The witnesses see in his being numbered with the transgressors the very heart of the atonement: the sinless One condemned with the guilty, that the guilty might be counted righteous in him. Barnes expounds Isaiah 53:12: not that Christ was a transgressor, but that in dying he had a place with them, dying the just for the unjust; the witnesses agree that, whatever the verse’s standing in the text, its meaning is the heart of the atonement, the sinless One condemned with the guilty that the guilty might be counted righteous in him. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, treats the two robbers, one a transgressor to the end, the other finding in his cross the beginning of restoration.
29:
‘And they that passed by railed on him, wagging their heads… Ah, thou that destroyest the temple.’ The witnesses note the mockery of the passers-by.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the interjection ‘Ah’ — ‘a kind of inarticulate scorn’ — is peculiar to Mark, ‘another instance of his habit of reproducing the very sounds.’ Barnes ties the head-wagging to Psalms 22:7. Gill notes they railed as they went by the cross.

The fathers turn the taunt against the mockers. Theophylact notes the devil moved them to bid him come down, knowing ‘that salvation was being won by the cross.’ Bede observes they confess against their will that ‘he saved many.’ Pseudo-Jerome presses the Jews: ‘how much more wonderful that a dead man should arise, than that one yet living should choose to come down from the cross.’

The shared, dominant note is that the passers-by mocked Christ with his own words about the temple, wagging their heads in scorn. The grammatical commentators note the fulfilment of the Psalm and Mark’s vivid reproduction of the jeer; the fathers see in the taunt a satanic design to lure him from the cross and so undo salvation. The witnesses agree that the very reproach — ‘thou that destroyest the temple’ — touched, unknowing, the deepest truth, for by the destroying of the temple of his body and its raising in three days he was even then accomplishing the redemption they mocked. Augustine reconciles the plural with Luke’s single reviler; the witnesses agree the passers-by mocked Christ with his own words about the temple, the fathers seeing in the taunt a satanic design to lure him from the cross, while the reproach touched unknowing the deepest truth, for by the destroying of the temple of his body and its raising in three days he was even then accomplishing redemption. Ellicott notes the interjection ‘Ah’ — a kind of inarticulate scorn — is peculiar to Mark, another instance of his habit of reproducing the very sounds that were uttered. Theophylact notes the devil moved them to bid him come down, knowing salvation was being won by the cross, and so proceeding to tempt Christ once more.
30:
‘Save thyself, and come down from the cross.’ The few voices note the taunt to descend.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the order of the clauses should be inverted, ‘come down from the cross, and save thyself.’ Gill notes the suggestion that, if he were what he claimed, he might easily free himself. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so few and brief relevant voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The mockers challenge Jesus to prove himself by coming down from the cross and saving himself — as though his remaining there disproved his claim. The taunt assumes that a true Son of God would never suffer such a death.

The shared, dominant note is the deep irony of the challenge. To ‘save himself’ by coming down would have been to abandon the very work for which he came; his not descending was not weakness but the strength of love and obedience. The witnesses see in the taunt the same temptation offered in the wilderness and through Peter — to grasp glory without the cross — and the supreme answer of Christ, who, able to save himself, chose rather to save others; for had he come down, he could have saved none, but by staying he saved the world. The mockers’ ‘save thyself’ is thus refuted by the gospel itself: he would not save himself, that he might save us. Ellicott notes the order of the clauses should be inverted, ‘come down from the cross, and save thyself,’ the taunt assuming a true Son of God would never suffer such a death. The witnesses see in the taunt the same temptation offered in the wilderness and through Peter, to grasp glory without the cross, which Christ refused for love.
31:
‘Likewise also the chief priests mocking said… He saved others; himself he cannot save.’ The single voice notes the rulers’ scornful triumph.

The Reformed Gill notes the chief priests mocked, ‘laughing at one another’ (so the Syriac), having gained their end and satiated their revenge on him.

With only one brief voice the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain. The chief priests, joining the rabble, mocked Jesus among themselves — ‘he saved others; himself he cannot save’ — gloating over their apparent victory. Their jeer granted, even in scorn, the truth of his past miracles: he had indeed saved others.

The shared, dominant note is the unwitting testimony hidden in the mockery. The priests meant to taunt his helplessness, but their words — ‘he saved others’ — confessed his works of mercy, and ‘himself he cannot save’ spoke a deeper truth than they knew: he could not save himself and save others too. The witnesses see in the rulers’ laughter both the depth of their malice, mocking a dying man, and the providence that made even their scorn proclaim the gospel — that the Saviour of others would not, for love, come down to save himself, but stayed upon the cross to finish the salvation they derided. The witnesses agree the priests’ jeer — ‘he saved others; himself he cannot save’ — confessed, even in scorn, the truth of his works of mercy, and spoke a deeper truth than they knew, that he could not save himself and save others too; the providence of God making even their laughter proclaim the gospel. Gill notes the chief priests mocked, laughing at one another, having gained their end and satiated their revenge, their jeer granting even in scorn the truth of his past miracles. The providence of God made even the priests’ laughter proclaim the gospel, that the Saviour of others would not, for love, come down to save himself. Gill notes the priests, having satiated their revenge, laughed among themselves, yet their jeer confessed even in scorn the truth of his works of mercy. The witnesses see in the rulers’ mockery the depth of their malice, gloating over a dying man, and the irony that ‘himself he cannot save’ was a deeper truth than they knew.
32:
‘Let Christ the King of Israel descend now from the cross, that we may see and believe.’ The few voices note the challenge and the railing of the crucified.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘now come down’ (now that he is nailed), and that ‘they use almost the very language of Jesus… words they had heard him use in his appeals to men to see and believe’; he ‘reproached him’ (imperfect, repeatedly), and notes Mark and Matthew omit the penitent robber of Luke 23:39-43. Ellicott marks ‘the Christ’ (the article emphatic). Clarke notes the addition ‘in him’ in some manuscripts.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the rulers challenge ‘the Christ, the King of Israel’ to come down that they may ‘see and believe,’ and even the crucified robbers reviled him. The grammatical commentators note the mocking echo of Christ’s own call to faith, now flung back at him in scorn.

The shared, dominant note is the false condition the mockers set for faith. They would believe, they say, if he came down — but a faith demanding such a sign is no faith, and would not have come had he descended. The witnesses see in this both the hardness of unbelief, which sets its own terms and will not be persuaded, and the wisdom of God, who gave a greater sign than they asked: not a coming down from the cross, but a rising from the grave. Had he saved himself, none could be saved; by not coming down, he opened to faith the sign of resurrection, that all who believe might see and live.
