Ellicott marks ‘the Christ’ (the article emphatic), the rulers using Christ’s own language of seeing and believing in mockery, a faith demanding such a sign being no faith at all.
33:
‘And when the sixth hour was come, there was darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour.’ The witnesses note the supernatural darkness over the dying Christ.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘the sixth hour’ is noon (Jewish time), the darkness lasting till three (Matthew 27:45). Theophylact stresses it was ‘the fourteenth moon, when no eclipse can take place,’ so the darkness was no natural eclipse. Henry ties it to the prophecy of the sun going down at noon (Amos 8:9).

The fathers read its meaning. Bede holds the light ‘took away its rays… lest it should see the Lord hanging,’ and notes that as Adam sinned after midday, so ‘the second Adam by dying destroyed death.’ Pseudo-Jerome and Augustine add mystical readings. Henry sees a sign of ‘the darkness… upon the Jewish church and nation.’

The shared, dominant note is that the three hours’ darkness was a supernatural sign attending the death of the Son of God. The grammatical commentators stress it could be no eclipse, the moon being full at Passover; the fathers and the Puritan read it as creation veiling its face at its Maker’s suffering and as a token of judgment on the unbelieving nation. The witnesses see in the noonday darkness both the horror of the hour — when the Father turned his face from the sin-bearing Son — and a cosmic witness that the one dying there was no mere man, the very heavens mourning the death by which the world was redeemed. Henry sees in the darkness a sign of the judgment coming on the Jewish church and nation; the witnesses agree the three hours’ darkness was a supernatural sign attending the death of the Son of God, creation veiling its face at its Maker’s suffering, the heavens mourning the death by which the world was redeemed. Theophylact stresses it was the fourteenth moon, when no eclipse can take place, so the darkness was no natural eclipse but a supernatural sign; Bede holds the light withdrew its rays lest it see the Lord hanging. Henry sees in the noonday darkness a sign of the judgment coming on the Jewish church and nation, the very heavens mourning the death by which the world was redeemed.
34:
‘Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani?… My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?’ The witnesses note the cry of dereliction from the Psalm.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the words. Lightfoot and Ellicott note Mark’s ‘Eloi’ (Aramaic/Chaldee) is ‘a closer reproduction of the very sounds’ than Matthew’s ‘Eli’ (Hebrew), both from Psalms 22:1. Barnes ties the cry to the same Psalm and Lamentations 1:12.

The Wesleyan Wesley expounds it: Christ ‘claiming God as his God, and yet lamenting his Father’s withdrawing the tokens of his love, and treating him as an enemy, while he bare our sins.’ Robertson (at v.33) notes the Father regarded him as sin (2 Corinthians 5:21), ‘the deepest suffering,’ yet he ‘did not cease to be the Son of God.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: from the darkness Jesus cried the opening words of Psalm 22, ‘My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?’ The grammatical commentators note Mark’s Aramaic form, and the Wesleyan and Reformed the awful reality — the sin-bearing Son experiencing the withdrawal of the Father’s comfortable presence.

The shared, dominant note is the unfathomable depth of the cry of dereliction. It was no loss of faith — he still says ‘my God’ — nor any breach of the eternal union, but the real bearing of our sin’s desert, the hiding of the Father’s face from the One made sin for us. The witnesses see in it both the price of redemption, the forsaking Christ endured that we might never be forsaken, and the fulfilment of the Psalm of the suffering and vindicated Messiah, whose cry of abandonment opens a song that ends in triumph. Robertson notes the Father regarded him as sin (2 Corinthians 5:21), the deepest suffering, yet he did not cease to be the Son of God; the witnesses see in the cry of dereliction no loss of faith — he still says ‘my God’ — nor any breach of the eternal union, but the real bearing of our sin’s desert, the forsaking Christ endured that we might never be forsaken.
35:
‘Behold, he calleth Elias.’ The two brief voices note the bystanders’ misunderstanding.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘they misunderstood the ‘Eloi’ or ‘Ele’ (my God) for Elijah.’ Gill notes it was ‘some of them that stood by’ the cross.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Some standing by, hearing the cry ‘Eloi,’ mistook it — or pretended to mistake it — for a call upon Elijah. The grammatical commentators note the likeness of the sounds that gave rise to the error.

The shared, dominant note is the misunderstanding, whether honest or mocking, of Christ’s dying cry. The Jews looked for Elijah to come (Malachi 4:5), and some, catching the sound ‘Eloi,’ supposed he called for the prophet’s help. The witnesses see in this either the ignorance of bystanders or a fresh taunt — waiting to see whether Elijah would come to save him — and in either case a token of the blindness that surrounded the cross, where the words of the Psalm of the Messiah were heard but not understood, and the dying Saviour was mocked even in his deepest agony. Gill notes it was some of those standing by the cross; the witnesses agree some, hearing the cry ‘Eloi,’ mistook or pretended to mistake it for a call upon Elijah, whom the Jews looked for (Malachi 4:5), a token of the blindness that surrounded the cross, where the words of the Psalm of the Messiah were heard but not understood. Robertson notes they misunderstood the ‘Eloi’ (my God) for Elijah, the likeness of the sounds giving rise to the error, whether of honest ignorance or fresh mockery, waiting to see if the prophet would come. The Jews looked for Elijah to come (Malachi 4:5), and some, catching the sound, supposed he called for the prophet’s help, a token of the blindness at the cross. Robertson notes the likeness of the sounds gave rise to the error, whether of honest ignorance or fresh mockery at the dying Saviour. The witnesses see in the misunderstanding, whether honest or mocking, a token of the blindness surrounding the cross, where the very words of the Messianic Psalm were heard but not understood.
36:
‘And one ran and filled a sponge full of vinegar… saying, Let alone; let us see whether Elias will come to take him down.’ The few voices note the vinegar and the taunt.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘to take him down’ where Matthew 27:49 has ‘to save him.’ Barnes ties the vinegar to Psalms 69:21. Gill notes Christ said ‘I thirst’ at this point (John 19:28). The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

The Reformed Calvin notes Mark gives the words to the one offering the vinegar, Matthew to the others; ‘there is no inconsistency… the jeering was begun by one… but eagerly seized by others.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: one ran to give Jesus vinegar on a sponge, while the crowd jeered, waiting to see if Elijah would come. The grammatical commentators note the fulfilment of the Psalm in the vinegar, and Calvin harmonises who spoke the taunt.

The shared, dominant note is the mingling of a small mercy with continued mockery. The vinegar — the sour wine of the soldiers — was offered to moisten his lips, yet the surrounding cry, ‘let us see whether Elijah will come,’ turned even this into derision. The witnesses see in the act both the fulfilment of Psalms 69:21, that they gave him vinegar to drink, and the persistence of unbelief, which watched for a miraculous rescue it did not expect, mocking to the last the Saviour who would not save himself but was even then finishing the work of redemption. Barnes ties the vinegar to Psalms 69:21, and Gill notes Christ said ‘I thirst’ at this point (John 19:28); the witnesses see in the act both a small mercy and continued mockery, the persistence of unbelief watching for a rescue it did not expect, mocking to the last the Saviour who would not save himself but was finishing redemption. Calvin notes Mark gives the words to the one offering the vinegar, Matthew to the others, the jeering begun by one and eagerly seized by the rest, with no inconsistency between the accounts.
37:
‘And Jesus cried with a loud voice, and gave up the ghost.’ The witnesses note the loud cry and the manner of his death.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘gave up the ghost’ is literally ‘breathed out’ (Matthew 27:50), the same verb used again of the centurion’s witness (v.39). Clarke notes it was about three o’clock, the ninth hour, ‘about the time the paschal lamb was usually sacrificed.’ Barnes and Wesley refer to the parallels.

Pseudo-Jerome (at v.33) observes that ‘we who are of the earth die with a very low voice… but he who descended from heaven breathed his last with a loud voice’ — a mark of his unspent strength. The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s death was marked by a great cry, a sign that he died not as exhausted nature fails but as one laying down his life by his own act.

The witnesses see in the loud cry the voluntariness of his death: no man took his life from him, but he gave it up (John 10:18). The timing too is significant — the ninth hour, when the evening lamb was offered — so that the true Paschal Lamb died at the hour of sacrifice. The witnesses agree that the manner of his dying, with strength enough for a loud cry, attested that he was no helpless victim but the willing sacrifice, who, having finished the work given him, dismissed his spirit and bowed his head in death. Clarke notes it was the ninth hour, about the time the paschal lamb was sacrificed; the witnesses see in the loud cry the voluntariness of his death — no man took his life from him, but he gave it up (John 10:18) — the true Paschal Lamb dying at the hour of sacrifice, no helpless victim but the willing sacrifice who, having finished his work, dismissed his spirit. Pseudo-Jerome notes that men of earth die with a low voice, but he who descended from heaven breathed his last with a loud voice, a mark of his unspent strength and the voluntariness of his death.
38:
‘And the veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom.’ The witnesses note the rending of the veil at Christ’s death and its meaning.

The fathers read its significance. Pseudo-Jerome: ‘the veil… is rent, that is, the heaven is opened’; the last become first, the Gentile confesses, the Jew denies. Theophylact reads the rending as the grace of the Spirit departing from the temple, the Holy of Holies laid open, and a figure of Christ’s flesh, the veil of his body, torn in his Passion (Hebrews 10:20). Bede and Augustine join the centurion’s confession.

The grammatical-historical Gill notes the rending ‘from the top to the bottom’ was accompanied by the earthquake and the opening of graves (Matthew 27:51). The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, dwells on the garden and the finished mystery.

The shared, dominant note is that the rent veil proclaimed the opening of the way to God through the death of Christ. Torn from top to bottom — by no human hand, but from heaven — it signified that the barrier between God and man was removed, the Holy of Holies thrown open, the old temple-order finished. The witnesses see in this the deepest meaning of the cross: the veil of Christ’s flesh rent that we might enter the holiest by his blood (Hebrews 10:19-20), and a sign of judgment on the temple and nation, whose sacred grace was now departed. Gill notes the rending from top to bottom was accompanied by the earthquake and the opening of graves (Matthew 27:51); the witnesses see in the rent veil the opening of the way to God through the death of Christ, the veil of his flesh torn that we might enter the holiest by his blood (Hebrews 10:19-20), and a sign of judgment on the temple whose grace was departed. Theophylact reads the rending as the grace of the Spirit departing from the temple, the Holy of Holies laid open, and a figure of Christ’s flesh, the veil of his body, torn in his Passion (Hebrews 10:20).
39:
‘Truly this man was the Son of God.’ The witnesses note the centurion’s confession at the manner of Christ’s death.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe him. Robertson notes ‘centurion’ is a Latin word, and that this description — standing ‘over against him,’ watching Jesus — is peculiar to Mark; the confession ‘so’ (with the darkness and earthquake) may be ‘a Son of God.’ Ellicott notes Mark uses the Latin word, Matthew the Greek (Matthew 27:54).

The fathers read the cause of his wonder. Bede notes the centurion marvelled that the Lord ‘sent forth his Spirit,’ for ‘no one can send forth his own spirit but he who is the Creator of souls.’ Augustine: the immediate yielding of the Spirit showed ‘no penalty of sin could have had power to cause the death of his flesh.’ The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the resurrection appearances, tangential.

The shared, dominant note is that a Gentile soldier, watching the death of Christ, was moved to confess him the Son of God. The grammatical commentators note Mark’s vivid picture of the watching centurion and the open question of the article; the fathers see the cause of his awe in the manner of the death — the loud cry, the voluntary yielding of the Spirit, the darkness and earthquake. The witnesses agree that the first confession at the foot of the cross came not from Israel but from a Roman, a first-fruit of the Gentiles and a witness that the crucified was indeed what the title proclaimed and the rulers denied. Ellicott notes Mark uses the Latin word, Matthew the Greek (Matthew 27:54); the witnesses agree the first confession at the foot of the cross came not from Israel but from a Roman, a first-fruit of the Gentiles and a witness that the crucified was indeed what the title proclaimed and the rulers denied, moved by the manner of his death. Bede notes the centurion marvelled that the Lord sent forth his Spirit, for no one can send forth his own spirit but he who is the Creator of souls, the first confession coming from a Roman, not from Israel.
40:
‘There were also women looking on afar off: among whom was Mary Magdalene… and Salome.’ The few voices note the faithful women at the cross.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify them. Ellicott notes the list matches Matthew 27:56, with ‘James the little’ and ‘Salome’ (only in Mark) for ‘the mother of Zebedee’s children’; Robertson identifies Salome as the mother of the sons of Zebedee. Clarke notes the reading Joses/Joseph. Gill notes they watched ‘at some distance from the cross.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: faithful women, who had followed Jesus, stood watching the crucifixion from a distance — Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joses, and Salome. The grammatical commentators harmonise the lists of the Gospels and note Salome as peculiar to Mark.

The shared, dominant note is the constancy of the women’s devotion. When the disciples had fled, these women remained, watching to the end, though only able to stand afar off. The witnesses see in their faithful presence both a rebuke to the cowardice of the men who had forsaken him and a testimony to the strength of love that would not abandon the Lord even in his death; these same women would be first at the tomb, and their steadfastness at the cross prepared them to be witnesses of the resurrection. Clarke notes the reading Joses or Joseph, and Gill that they watched at some distance from the cross; the witnesses see in the women’s faithful presence both a rebuke to the cowardice of the men who had fled and a testimony to the strength of love, these same women being first at the tomb, their steadfastness preparing them to witness the resurrection. Ellicott notes the list matches Matthew 27:56, with ‘James the little’ and ‘Salome’ (only in Mark) for ‘the mother of Zebedee’s children,’ the women watching to the end though only from afar. Robertson identifies Salome as the mother of the sons of Zebedee, the women’s faithful presence rebuking the cowardice of the men who had fled. Gill notes they watched at some distance from the cross, observing what was said and done, faithful to the end though only able to stand afar off.
41:
‘Who also, when he was in Galilee, followed him, and ministered unto him.’ The few voices note the women’s long service to Christ.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the two imperfects — ‘followed’ and ‘ministered’ — describing ‘the long Galilean ministry of these three women and many other women’ (Luke 8:1-3) who came up with him to Jerusalem; ‘these faithful women were last at the Cross.’ Barnes ties their ministry to the support they gave him (Luke 8:3); Wesley notes they ‘provided him with necessaries.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: these women had followed and served Jesus throughout his Galilean ministry and had come with him to Jerusalem. The grammatical commentators note the imperfect tenses marking a sustained devotion, and that many other women shared in this faithful service.

The shared, dominant note is the long faithfulness and practical love of the women who served Christ. From Galilee to the cross they had followed and ministered to him out of their substance, and now stood by him in death when his chosen apostles had fled. The witnesses see in their steadfast service both an example of grateful discipleship — ministering to Christ in his earthly need — and the providence that kept them near to the cross and the tomb, so that the women who had served him living were honoured to attend him in death and to be the first heralds of his rising. Barnes ties their ministry to the support they gave him out of their substance (Luke 8:3); the witnesses see in their long faithfulness an example of grateful discipleship and the providence that kept them near the cross and the tomb, so that the women who served him living were honoured to attend him in death and to be the first heralds of his rising. Robertson notes the two imperfects ‘followed’ and ‘ministered’ describe the long Galilean ministry of these women (Luke 8:1-3) who came up with him to Jerusalem and were last at the cross.
42:
‘And now when the even was come, because it was the preparation, that is, the day before the sabbath.’ The witnesses note the timing of the burial on the eve of the sabbath.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the term. Robertson and Ellicott note ‘the preparation’ (parasceue) is explained by Mark for Gentile readers as ‘the day before the sabbath,’ our Friday, the Jews having already moved to remove the bodies (John 19:31). Lightfoot expounds the rabbinic sense of preparing food for the sabbath (Exodus 16:23). Barnes and Clarke note the haste before sunset, the law forbidding bodies to hang overnight.

The fathers read the day mystically. Bede notes that, as man was made on the sixth day and God rested on the seventh, fitly was the Saviour crucified on the sixth and rested in the tomb on the sabbath, awaiting the Resurrection on the eighth day. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

The shared, dominant note is that the approach of the sabbath pressed for a hasty burial, and so prepared the way for Joseph’s bold request. The grammatical commentators stress Mark’s explanation of the Jewish term for his Gentile readers, and the legal urgency that the body not remain on the cross. The witnesses see in the timing both the providence that gave Christ an honourable burial before the feast and, with Bede, the fitness of the Lord’s resting in the tomb on the sabbath, as having finished the work of the new creation. Lightfoot expounds the rabbinic sense of preparing food for the sabbath (Exodus 16:23); the witnesses agree the approach of the sabbath pressed for a hasty burial and so prepared the way for Joseph’s bold request, and, with Bede, the fitness of the Lord’s resting in the tomb on the sabbath, having finished the work of the new creation. Bede notes that, as man was made on the sixth day and God rested on the seventh, fitly was the Saviour crucified on the sixth day and rested in the tomb on the sabbath, awaiting the eighth-day Resurrection. Robertson notes the Jews had already taken steps to remove the bodies (John 19:31), the law forbidding bodies to hang overnight on the sabbath.
43:
‘Joseph of Arimathaea, an honourable counsellor, which also waited for the kingdom of God, came, and went in boldly unto Pilate, and craved the body of Jesus.’ The witnesses note Joseph’s rank, hope, and courage.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe him. Robertson notes ‘a councillor of honourable estate’ (a member of the Sanhedrin, rich, Matthew 27:57), ‘looking for the Kingdom of God’ (the verb used of Simeon, Luke 2:25), a secret disciple (John 19:38) who now ‘became bold.’ Lightfoot expounds the ‘chamber of the counsellors.’ Clarke marks that ‘he who was a coward before now acts a more open, fearless part than any of the disciples.’

The Reformed Calvin holds the highest praise is that he ‘waited for the kingdom of God,’ ‘the root and source of his righteousness.’ The fathers and Wesley note his rank and expectation. Theophylact marks his ‘praiseworthy boldness,’ not fearing to fall from his rich estate.

The shared, dominant note is the courage of a hitherto secret disciple. Joseph, a man of rank and a member of the very council that condemned Jesus, who had waited for the kingdom of God, now boldly went to Pilate to beg the body when the apostles had fled. The witnesses see in his sudden boldness both the working of grace, which made the timid brave, and the providence that secured for Christ an honourable burial — the rich man’s tomb of Isaiah 53:9 — through one whose faith, long hidden, blazed forth at the darkest hour. Lightfoot expounds the ‘chamber of the counsellors’; the witnesses see in Joseph’s sudden boldness both the working of grace, which made the timid brave, and the providence that secured for Christ an honourable burial — the rich man’s tomb of Isaiah 53:9 — through one whose faith, long hidden, blazed forth at the darkest hour. Clarke marks that he who was a coward before now acts a more open, fearless part than any of the disciples, the death of Jesus becoming the grand cause of confidence and courage to his servants. Calvin holds the highest praise of Joseph is that he waited for the kingdom of God, which was the root and source of his righteousness.
44:
‘And Pilate marvelled if he were already dead.’ The few voices note Pilate’s surprise at the swiftness of Christ’s death.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the wonder. Robertson notes the classical idiom (‘wonder if’), and that death by crucifixion was usually ‘lingering’; this item and Pilate’s calling the centurion are ‘only in Mark.’ Barnes notes the crucified often lived two or three days, even six or seven; Joseph’s request implied Jesus was dead. Ellicott notes the article points to the same centurion of v.39.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Pilate, surprised that Jesus had died so soon, would not grant the body until he had made sure, calling the centurion to confirm it. The grammatical commentators note that this detail, peculiar to Mark, both attests the reality of Christ’s death and shows Pilate’s care to verify it officially.

The shared, dominant note is the official confirmation of Christ’s death. Because crucifixion usually killed slowly, Pilate marvelled and required the centurion’s witness before releasing the body. The witnesses see in this a providential safeguard against later denials: the death of Jesus was no swoon but a fact certified by the Roman officer in charge, so that the reality on which the resurrection rests was attested by the very authority that had crucified him. Barnes notes the crucified often lived two or three days, even six or seven, so Joseph’s request implied Jesus was dead; the witnesses see in this detail, peculiar to Mark, both an attestation of the reality of Christ’s death and Pilate’s care to verify it officially, a safeguard against later denials that the death was no swoon but a fact certified by Rome. Robertson notes the classical idiom ‘wonder if,’ and that this item and Pilate’s calling the centurion are only in Mark, both attesting the reality of the death and Pilate’s care to verify it. Barnes notes the crucified often lived two or three days, so Joseph’s request, implying Jesus was dead, moved Pilate to verify it by the centurion in charge. Ellicott notes the article points to the same centurion of the confession, whose official word confirmed the reality of the death against all later denial.
45:
‘And when he knew it of the centurion, he gave the body to Joseph.’ The few voices note the granting of the body once death was confirmed.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘granted the corpse’ (ptoma) is the only place this word is applied to Jesus’ body, and that Pilate was ‘probably glad to turn the body over to Joseph,’ else it would go to the potter’s field. Barnes notes the centurion, in charge of the watch, could give correct information. Gill notes the soldiers’ report that he was truly dead, his side pierced (John 19:34).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: once the centurion confirmed the death, Pilate granted the body to Joseph. The grammatical commentators note the official certification and that Pilate, satisfied, freely released the corpse for honourable burial.

The shared, dominant note is that the death of Christ, officially confirmed, opened the way for his burial by a friend. Pilate’s grant of the body to Joseph, on the centurion’s word, both rests on the certainty of the death and rescues the Saviour from a criminal’s common grave. The witnesses see in this the providence that fulfilled the Scripture — that he should make his grave with the rich (Isaiah 53:9) — and the honour God put on his Son even in death, the body that bore our sins committed to reverent hands for a worthy tomb. Gill notes the soldiers’ report that he was truly dead, his side pierced (John 19:34); the witnesses see in Pilate’s grant of the body the providence that fulfilled the Scripture — that he should make his grave with the rich (Isaiah 53:9) — and the honour God put on his Son even in death, the body committed to reverent hands for a worthy tomb. Robertson notes ‘granted the corpse’ (ptoma) is the only place this word is applied to Jesus’ body, Pilate probably glad to give it to Joseph rather than let it go to the potter’s field.
46:
‘And he bought fine linen… wrapped him in the linen, and laid him in a sepulchre… and rolled a stone unto the door.’ The witnesses note the reverent burial in the rock-hewn tomb.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe it. Robertson notes the rare verbs for ‘wound’ the body in the linen, with the spices Nicodemus brought (John 19:39-40), laid in Joseph’s new rock-hewn tomb; the work was hurried before the sabbath. Ellicott notes the buying of the linen just before the sabbath is peculiar to Mark. Wesley notes the stone was rolled ‘by his servants,’ too large for one.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, dwells on the rock-hewn tomb foretold by the prophets. With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: Joseph, with reverent haste, wrapped the body in fresh linen and laid it in a new tomb cut in the rock, sealing it with a great stone.

The shared, dominant note is the honourable and careful burial of the Lord. Though done in haste before the sabbath, it was an act of love and reverence — the fine linen, the new tomb, the sealing stone. The witnesses see in the rock-hewn sepulchre both the fulfilment of prophecy and the dignity God gave his Son in death; and in the great stone rolled to the door, the providence that would make the empty tomb, sealed and guarded, the unanswerable witness to the resurrection that no enemy could explain away. Robertson notes the body was wound in the linen with the spices Nicodemus brought (John 19:39-40), the work hurried before the sabbath; the witnesses see in the rock-hewn sepulchre both the fulfilment of prophecy and the dignity God gave his Son, and in the great stone the providence that would make the sealed and guarded tomb the unanswerable witness to the resurrection. Ellicott notes the buying of the linen just before the sabbath is peculiar to Mark, and Wesley that the stone was rolled by his servants, being too large for one man to move. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note, on point, dwells on the rock-hewn tomb foretold by the prophets, in which the Lord was laid.
47:
‘And Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses beheld where he was laid.’ The witnesses note the women who watched the burial.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect ‘beheld’ — the two Marys ‘sitting over against the sepulchre’ (Matthew 27:61) and ‘watching the actions of Joseph and Nicodemus’; they ‘constituted themselves a party of observation,’ seeing where the body was laid. Ellicott notes she is ‘the other Mary’ of Matthew.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the lesson: ‘a timid man and a few weak women acknowledge Jesus in death, when the strong… forsook him’; ‘the weakest woman can do all things, if Christ strengthen her.’ Barnes notes that ‘female love never forsook him,’ flourishing ‘in the dark shades of adversity.’

The shared, dominant note is the faithful watching of the women, which both honoured the buried Christ and prepared them to be witnesses of the resurrection. Having seen exactly where he was laid, they could return after the sabbath and find the tomb empty, knowing it was the right one. The witnesses see in their steadfast love a rebuke to the fled disciples and a providential link between the cross and the empty tomb: the same eyes that saw the stone rolled to the door would be among the first to find it rolled away, so that the women last at the burial were first at the rising, the unbroken chain of their devoted attention sealing the truth of both. Barnes notes that female love never forsook him, flourishing in the dark shades of adversity; the witnesses see in the women’s watching a providential link between the cross and the empty tomb, the same eyes that saw the stone rolled to the door being first to find it rolled away, so that the women last at the burial were first at the rising. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘beheld’ pictures the two Marys sitting over against the sepulchre (Matthew 27:61), constituting themselves a party of observation, seeing exactly where the body was laid. Clarke draws the lesson that a timid man and a few weak women acknowledged Jesus in death when the strong forsook him, for the weakest can do all things if Christ strengthen her.
